comments.body: Tennis court right? Try doing them running parallel to the lines on the court, it's great help to get them on lock. It acts as something to aim your trick at. Keep it up!
You could do it with a bit more practice, you’re nearly there.

Keep it up!

Also... I absolutely love your hair  :D
Try turning earlier, before you even bring the front wheels up, helped me
Damn gurrrll
Try to pop straight down
https://youtu.be/p7GRG5DCTxY
changed my whole perspective THANK U
Try doing it on fakie, for some reason that worked for me, that and start the rotation on the shoulders a little bit earlier than the pop

How long it takes to learn something can be influenced by a number of factors. For instance, how long you've been playing, how many hours you spend practicing, the effectiveness of the exercises you do, whether you can already play simmular pieces to the one you want to learn and I could go on. 

If it helps give you an idea of how long it will take you to learn the Rosanna shuffle, it took me about a month of casually playing the groove after learning it to get it to a point where I was happy with it. I've been playing for 10 years, spend 5-9 hours a week practicing and could already play the groove to Fool in the Rain.

Hope that helps.
Technically, not very long. Spiritually, the rest of your life.
How long is a piece of string?

Don't ask how long it will take to learn something that you want to learn. Just get to work. How long it takes for you will depend on many factors like how hard you work and including how quickly you generally learn things.

Thanks a lot, I've already learnt fools in the rain (a wonderful track might I add) but the Rosanna shuffle has been bothering me for 3-4 weeks and it's a bit baffling since I don't take this long to learn a song, and I have trouble in keeping the beat throughout the song like my high hi hats sound from the proper triplets to a 4/4 for some reason. I don't know if you understood but yeah

Thanks for the advice 👍

The difference between being able to perfectly nail fool in the rain and the rosanna shuffle is speed- and if you can’t maintain the speed needed for rosanna, it could mean that the hand technique you’re using for fool might not be the same technique you need for rosanna. 

Fool in the rain, for me, can be played using mostly the wrist- but for a tempo like rosanna, I have to rely more on stick rebound, using less wrist motion. This might be where your issue is.

What grip/technique are you using?

Same, I use the wrist for fool in the rain, but with the same wrist motion I'm struggling for the Rosanna shuffle. I shall try more on stick rebound tho 👍

I use the German grip, and I use 2 techniques like for example for spirit of the radio I use my fingers but for songs which have a swing like rhythm I use my wrist.
i definitely feel like i'm still finding things to improve about this and similar grooves. so, a lifetime?
5 minutes

submission.title: My hihat stand isn't going down - help 😭

comments.body:
I want to help but I'm not sure what you mean. I'm wondering though if all you need to do is loosen the wingnut on the clutch (the part holding the top hi-hat cymbal), then push the pedal down an inch or two and then tighten the wingnut.

Edit: If deanjdavies is correct, then use the wingnut to tighten the pull rod so that it doesn't come loose again so easily.

My guess is the internal connection between the top post and the pedal has come apart. Take the hats off and turn the post clockwise until it's tight. Then put hats back on and tighten the lock nut as usual to select how far open you want them.
A video of you using the pedal would help us see exactly what's going on. Also point the camera under the high hat during the video.
You seem to have placed a chair on top of your hihat. Remove the chair safely, and it should go down
Sometimes you gotta treat her right and buy her dinner first.
Just keep fiddling around with it mate, how everybody learns their way around the kit
So like the hi hat is too high for me and it's not going down and it's weird considering I've had a previous stand that could go down but this one doesn't
Push harder… never fails lol

Okay, so the real issue here is that the bottom cymbal isn’t able to be lowered enough for your liking?

The wingnut that’s about halfway down the stand should allow you to telescope the rod on which the bottom cymbal rests further down into the outer rod, thus lowering the bottom cymbal; if said inner rod cannot be lowered any further, then you’ve likely hit the lowest setting on the stand, in which case options are:
- find a stand with a greater range of adjustability
- find a stand from a youth set
- raise the throne so you sit higher, making the hi-hat more comfortable to use.
There is one more option, but I would only do it if you are sure and at least somewhat competent with this type of thing: pull the top pipe out and cut it.
This is a great suggestion.

submission.title: Rosanna shuffle - it just won't sound right... It's been 2 months since I've been working on this song, would love some advice cause I'm self taught

comments.body: I would find someone who plays a cover of the song on YouTube a study their technique!

The most important missing part is the backbeat. Dont be arfraid to crack a rimshot on 2 and 4.

Edit: backbeat is on 3 here, brainfarted for a second.
Backbeat is on 3 here, it's a half time shuffle
My bad, went into auto mode. Point still stands. Backbeat needs to be stronger because now all the snare notes sound the same. Thanks.

You just need to be looser and watch your dynamics.

Play it slower, half or three quarters your current tempo, and really focus on the difference between the ghost notes and rim shots on the snare.

Your note placement is pretty good, but it's still throwing you a bit, it should be chill, but I can still hear that you're putting effort into it.

You're doing good tho, it's a tricky one, you just need to get the feel right

You’re getting there. The ghost notes are a little inconsistent (not always adhering to the triplet grid) and some of the kick notes aren’t always placed right. Slow it down. Get the triplet grid really solid, get more comfortable with mixing up accents and taps on the snare, and get those kick notes placed right. Then speed it back up gradually.
Jeff Porcaro did a clinic at Musicians Institute back in 88 and the is a clip on the tube of him talking about this
True

Echoing what else has been said, slow way down. Practice with a metronome is essential. Pay attention to where the notes are happening in the triplet grid, get the muscle memory happening correctly and really differentiate between the ghost strokes and the backbeat. Practice it this way for a painfully long, tedious and boring amount of time (hours and hours) until it feels as easy to you as breathing, then practice some more. Only then should you bring up the tempo. Once you put in this work, you'll find it's incredibly easier to play at tempo and sounds great because the foundation is solid. There's no quick or easy way. It just takes putting in the time, and being able to push through the discomfort of this kind of practice is what separates the good players from the mediocre. I should know; it took me a lot of years of being mediocre to understand this.

Stop trying to learn the rosanna shuffle, and aim for Fool in the Rain by zeppelin instead. 

It’s the same thing, at a more comfortable pace, and a slightly different flavor. Your rosanna shuffle will thank you for it later.

Until you fix your grip, it will be difficult to play this shuffle at all, let alone for the entire duration of the track. Grip technique is too big of a topic here but this is a problem every drummer faces. Fix this and a lot of seemingly difficult patterns will become easy to tackle.  


Currently your thumbs are resting on the side of the stick. This is the first thing you'll want to address. Good luck in your journey.
Purdy shuffle is above my pay grade. Breaks my brain to try it.
[deleted]
Shakespeare-Bot, thou hast been voted most annoying bot on Reddit. I am exhorting all mods to ban thee and thy useless rhetoric so that we shall not be blotted with thy presence any longer.
Practicing Accent taps will help your left wrist get up in preparation for the backbeat.

I noticed youre skipping the snare ghost note right after each shot. I hate to say it but ya gotta keep that note in there. It's super noticeable when it's missing[Here's the chart](https://images.app.goo.gl/RyLQHgUsiJ25skg28), it's the 8th charted note in the first full measure

I have the same type of problem.  I still have trouble, but this has helped a lot:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NGqaSfFcJBU&ab_channel=RobBrown

It's different basic shuffles, but includes the Rosanna shuffle.

Nice try!

the biggest thing is to practice it waaay slower. at the moment your triplets are quite inconsistent, and there are some coordination issues between your hands and feet.

My advice would be to try playing rlr rlr rlr rlr (or lrl lrl lrl lrl as it looks like you are left handed) with a metronome set to maybe 120bpm (or however slow to feel comfortable). really focus on ghosting your left hand. keep your left (or right) hand super relaxed and literally just let the stick fall on the drum from a height maybe 1 inch above it or less. the stick will pretty much rebound back up on its own. if you have a fancy metronome you should also be able to set it to triplet subdivisions to help you align your beats to it. once you get super comfortable with this start adding in the backbeat on 3 again. make sure this doesn't throw your ghost notes off and make sure the backbeat is not too quiet compared to the rest of it.  Its also worth practicing the bass drum part on its own slowly with a metronome too.

The next thing is to put the whole thing back together again really slowly (I always tell people to practice things like this at the point where the tempo crosses from sounding like a series of disconnected notes into something that actually grooves if that makes sense). Only once you can do this in your sleep should you increase the metronome. do this in gradual increments of maybe 5-10bpm.

Keep at it like that and before you know it you will have it down!

Two videos that helped me are this one where Porcaro explains the part himself (notice how low his left hand is for the ghosts) :

[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NMI81yIlT0Q&ab\_channel=SamJohnson](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NMI81yIlT0Q&ab_channel=SamJohnson)

And this, which is the isolated drum part from the song which I found helpful to play along to and is also a masterclass in feel and dynamics:

[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DQ-gfb6nUus&ab\_channel=LILIANESCALANTETRIVE%C3%91O](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DQ-gfb6nUus&ab_channel=LILIANESCALANTETRIVE%C3%91O)

​

Do you know the Purdie shuffle? That's often a good starting point for the Rosanna shuffle because its very similar and a lot slower and the kick drum part is less complicated. listen to songs like "Home at last" and "Babylon sisters" by Steely Dan.

Don't go hard on yourself with it, its definitely not an easy part to learn at all. just break it down into its individual parts, play it slowly, and gradually speed it up. Its one of those ones you could spend a lifetime trying to replicate and never get it just like Porcaro's.

Hope this helped and good luck!
Keep playing it slowly
I've been working on this for 20 years and it still doesn't sound quite right.. So hard!
Yes, backbeat stronger. (But not rimshot- rimshot too stong for that mellow Purdy Shuffle feel).

I am still learning this, too. Takes forever. Left hand grip looks good, right hand is way too tight. Also, I agree with others here that you have to practice super slow, and you might be doing that already. 

I suggest first doing ghost notes only. Ignore the big snare note until all the other pieces fall into place.

2 months, huh? Just 118 more months and you'll be pretty close!

Keep at it and don't forget to have fun.
So what grip do you suggest for this?
I started off with the Purdie shuffle and then proceeded with this, but I'm definitely thankful for your suggestion!
Do you mean the ghost note after the snare hit?

I learned this by removing elements.  Get solid on each part the snare ghosts and backbeats are the key (at least for me).  Get them down slowly alone maybe play with a track.  There is a program called Audacity - it's free and you can slow things down and get a better grip.  Good Luck, you'll get there - perseverance is the key.
yes

The grip style you choose is up to you (I play using the French grip) but generally you're going to be using your thumb and index finger as fulcrums to gain control, speed, dynamics, etc ... This article is a good intro into grips and references several fantastic resources from Jim Chapin, Dave Weckl, and more that I highly recommend diving into. Good luck!

https://drummagazine.com/thomas-lang-says-get-a-grip/

one thing no one else has mentioned, in the Rosanna shuffle the bass drum beat in the second bar is on the + of 1 not on the 2. so the exact beat would be wrong. other than that just slow it down to get the overall feel and timing better.

Your technique is working against you.

1. Get the thumbs perfectly centered on the sticks.
2. Get the stick **out** of your hands, not up into them.
3. Get the sticks only on the fingertips of your middle, ring, and pinky fingers and never let ANY of them come off the sticks, especially the pinky because it's the 2nd strongest finger next to the thumb. These 3 fingers work together as a team to help maximize your speed, power, control, and endurance. Your comfort is maximized as well. You can look at these 3 fingers as a single unit. Keep the fingertips of these 3 fingers in contact with the sticks at all times.
4. Keep the index fingers completely relaxed/limp. This will enable you to use the middle finger fulcrum.

With all 4 of these things in place, you can hold the sticks loosely and gently without any problems. In addition to that, you won't have to work as hard to make the sticks move because they will be free to move quite a bit on their own in your hands from smaller hand movements. The sticks should be able to swivel freely in the middle finger fulcrum.

The only time you need to let the index fingers be a part of the grip is when doing finger control or when playing very softly and quietly. The Double Stroke Roll is another thing that requires the assistance of the index fingers, so this also includes any rudiment that contains RR or LL.
Do you have any video help? Because I'm getting confused with the 2nd point but thanks for the long detailed suggestion really appreciate it

Let the sticks be on the fingertips of the middle, ring and pinky fingers but don't put the sticks close to the palm/inside of your hands. You should be able to put another stick on top of the stick in your hand if you use the perspective of having the palms down. It should be nice and open.

My life isn't letting me have time to do videos right now. I can recommend my super-long instructional video though. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=X3CF1zvMOYY

When I recorded it, I tried to cover everything I could think of that can affect good technique in addition to just the lesson itself on technique.
If it’s set on 3 than isn’t it not half time? Half time would be still calling it 2 and 4 but halving the tempo, while setting it on 3 is keeping the same tempo. Am I mistaken?
Rimshot with just the top quarter or so of the stick

Technically “half time” would imply a backbeat on beat 3. Regular time grooves on 2 and 4, so half time is on 3. But that sort of technicality is really only important when it’s notated down, or being conducted etc…

In reality it’s just a feel. Where you want to feel it is down to the player, and where you want to hear it is down to the listener. This same groove being played by OP could be considered “half time” with 8th triplets and a backbeat on 3, or a slow “regular time”, with 16th triplets and backbeats on 2 and 4. Dealer’s choice.

I’ve always just framed it as 16th trips/snare on 2+4 and half tempo. Obv doesn’t really matter which way, especially bc when playing I just feel the notes as relative rates to one another without a necessary label. I guess more importantly I was saying the original commentor didn’t make a mistake.

Your motion in the right hand looks pretty good and has to potential to really get the shuffle swinging. That big wide open back of the hand is causing some issues however. The fingers are completely off the stick at all times, so you’ve got no control over the stick, and it’s free to bounce around all over. There’s a time to open up the grip, but you need to close again for the control (/down) stroke, to keep the stick low and in position for the following upstroke. There should be a quarter note accent (or 8th note if you’re feeling it with a 2&4 backbeat) which drives the shuffle forward, creating the right feel. As others have said, this should be practiced super slowly at first.

Yeah totally agree that the original commentator didn’t make a mistake, it can be felt both ways and the guy who corrected him was being far too pedantic (or possibly just doesn’t know it could be felt either way).

Just to clarify re: your edit; clarification really not needed mate, and you didn’t brainfart at all. It’s perfectly acceptable to feel this groove with either a single backbeat on 3, or backbeats on 2 and 4. It just depends where you’re feeling the pulse, but unless it’s notated out in front of you that’s entirely subjective to the player and listener.

It can be felt either way *only* if you're ignoring the rest of the band. If you're listening to the rest of the band, then it's very clear where the one is, and the rest of the beats. 

If you ignore the rest of the music you could write it in a load of other ways that would be technically correct, but if you actually listen to the song, it's a 4/4 half time shuffle, the backbeat is on three, not two or four, that's not pedantic, it's a fact.

If you're giving someone advice on how to play it, it's important to deal with the actual music, not some abstraction.

Yeah you're mistaken, "half time" means you keep the same tempo but double the duration of the main/accent notes, so you keep the one on one, but the backbeat goes to 3, and you only get one backbeat per bar.

The commenter was correct in the point they were making, but just off about where the notes are. It's not that big a deal, but if someone's asking for advice that advice should be clear imo

And what music were we ignoring in OP’s post here exactly? I’m also not convinced that makes sense even when there is music around the groove. In something like Rosanna for example, since the bo-diddly pattern on the kick is where the phrase resolves, why exactly would it be ignoring the music if someone were to feel the start of that phrase as beat one, as opposed to beat one being halfway through the resolve?

It seems like it’d be more productive to teach that it doesn’t matter which way because they are the same relative to themselves. I always understood the other way (back beat 2+4) when learning, so depending on the person it might be more clear to frame it as that. It seems saying that it is “officially” one way or the other makes it harder to feel it for what it actually is, so either way would be clear imo.

Sorry for being picky though, obviously doesn’t really matter, but that’s my point.

You're ignoring the rest of the music in the song. Have a listen to it without drums, the beats are pretty clear.

If you're not trying to work out the possible ways you could write it, it's 4/4 at about 80bpm. If you want to count the beats as 8ths you can, but that would make it 8 8th note beats to the bar, and this isn't 8/8, it's clearly 4/4. You could call it 12/8 too if you wanted, and it'd be less of a stretch, but it's not, it's 4/4.

There's absolutely no reason why a phrase should last one bar, this type of phrase is called an across-the-bar phrase, it's very common. You orient yourself by where the beats are, not by where a phrase resolves, or where the snare is.

Sorry buddy but you’re really misunderstanding a lot here, and you’re actually contradicting yourself, and at the same time proving my point perfectly.

I never ever said it wasn’t 4/4. Both versions with either back beat on 3, or backbeats on 2 and 4, are both felt in 4/4.

In your own words, you’ve said Rosanna is 4/4 at 80bpm. Perfect, that’s fine if that’s how you want to feel it, but go have a listen to Rosanna now with your metronome set with quarter notes at 80bpm, and tell me where the back beats fall. Because surprise, that gives you two backbeats per bar, on 2 and 4!

If you want to feel Rosanna classically “half time”, it would still be 4/4, but at approx 160bpm. That would give you the single back on beat 3. And again, either is fine. Neither ignores or goes against the music around Rosanna, or any other song in this feel. They’re both valid ways of approaching the same song or groove. One just has the pulse set at double the time.

I’m really not going to spend all night arguing with you on this. But please believe me that, with a great deal of respect, I understand more about this concept than you do. I do it for a living, and have done for a long time. Rather than shutting me down because it doesn’t line up with how you currently view it, take it as a learning opportunity and a chance to understand more about the feel.

The problem is we're talking about conventions.

Back beat in 4/4 is on 2 and 4. Half time, whatever it might sound like, means that the feel is half of the actual tempo.

Once you put in "depending on the person" you're on the wrong track, because the whole point is to have a common terminology where everyone knows what's meant.

People who don't know the conventions might think that's an awkward way to do it, and maybe it even is, but it doesn't matter, the convention is what it is.

Google "drums half time shuffle" and I promise you you'll get the Purdy shuffle, the Bonham shuffle, and the Rosanna shuffle.

And as I mentioned to the other commenter, listen to the song without drums, it's clear where the beats are, the way you're counting it, that would make this an 8/8 song, but it isn't, it's 4/4

Here's Jeff Pocaro, the guy who wrote it explaining it as a half time shuffle [https://www.facebook.com/watch/?v=2884284021650243](https://www.facebook.com/watch/?v=2884284021650243)

I googled the tempo, so I might've got it off, but listen to it without drums [https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XwWqsrMQyfE&ab\_channel=C3444BX](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XwWqsrMQyfE&ab_channel=C3444BX)

Either it's 4/4 and a halftime or there are 8 beats to the bar

I’m not denying that it’s a half time shuffle, never have. I’m also not denying it’s in 4/4, again never have. This is where you’re misunderstanding completely. The point is, the half time shuffle can be felt at two rates of speed. Both are 4/4, and both are equally valid.

The tempo which YOU and apparently Google stated, at 80bpm, makes it 4/4 - four beats to a bar, with back beats on two and four. If you consider the bo diddly kick drum pattern (which Porcaro explains in that video) to be one completed loop of the groove, in this instance the hi hat notes are counted:

**1** e & **a** *2* e **&** a 3 **e** & a **4** e & a

I’ve made where the kick drum falls bold.

The alternative tempo which works just as well, would be 160bpm (exactly twice as fast). Again, still 4/4, and still four beats to a bar. The bo diddly kick pattern now takes two bars to resolve, but that’s also fine. The count is now

**1** & 2 **&** *3* & **4** & | 1 **&** 2 & **3** & 4 &

Both are half time shuffles. Both are correct. Both work with the music. Both of them have beat 1 in the correct place. Both are 4/4. Both have 4 beats in the bar. Neither of them are ‘abstract’ or go against the song. Neither goes against anything that Porcaro or Purdie say in their explanations. It’s literally just how a performer or listener is choosing to subdivide what they’re hearing.

You’re trying to “school” me somehow, but you don’t understand I have devoted my life to studying this stuff. I literally have a degree in drumming, have made a good living from performing the last 15 years all over the world, and have taught this very groove concept at music schools, to very advanced students countless times. Ask yourself, do you really think it’s me who after all this has missed something here and doesn’t have full grasp of this concept? Or is it more likely you still have a little bit to learn on it?
What beat is the backbeat in a halftime shuffle?

So you’re just not listening to anything at all now? **It depends what rate you’re feeling it at**. The backbeat on a half time shuffle can be considered to be either beat 3, or on both 2 and 4.

Tell me, if you do understand how to feel it on 2 and 4, what exactly goes “against the music”
In this instance? What are you saying doesn’t make it fit, which makes it so incorrect in your eyes?

I'm not listening to you saying you're right, because you're great no.

The way you describe it there's no distinction between half time and normal, but there is.

You're not talking about feeling it on 2 and 4, you're feeling it on the 3, and calling the consecutive threes 2 and 4.

Again, listen to the song without drums, what you're calling a bar of 4/4 has 8 beats. That's not 4/4

I’m saying relying on the construct of a convention, while useful for learning, is reductive of the art as a whole. The convention is “what it is,” but so is the pattern relative to itself, no matter how it is conceptualized. Even if others are framing the way I explain it as 8/8, and it would be given the intended framework provided by the writer, I could be conceptualizing my own playing and the rest of the song to still be in 4/4 regardless of whatever convention. The convention is still something subjectively reached even if every drummer learns and confides in it and limits their conceptualization to it. It wouldn’t be wrong that way either, but just because someone calls it something different wouldn’t make them wrong, as their own convention is as equally subjectively valid.

Believe me, I will have listened to that song without drums more than you have done. It was the subject of about a months worth of dedicated classes and study at university.

It doesn’t have 8 beats at all dude. Still four beats both ways. Just count the beats at half the rate you were counting before. If anything it’s just a more relaxed and laid back way to feel it.

And I’m not being rhetorical here. Actually do it. Put your metronome on to 80bpm (as you stated) and count 1, 2, 3, 4 along with that pulse, and tell me where the backbeats fall.

Then if you like, count twice as fast. They both work, and they’re both fine.

Of course a metronome at half the speed won't be out, if you only count two bars. If you ever have a section with five bars all of a sudden you're playing half bars. Or if you just count one.

You're imposing your count on the song instead of counting it.

As I already said, I'm not talking about every possible permutation I'm talking in the context of a reddit thread where someone asked for advice, and trying to make that advice as clear and useful as possible.

You're free to think of it however you want to, but communication requires a common frame of reference, and the conventions I'm using are that, that's why I'm using them

It would be the simplest thing in the world *if* that “5 bar phrase” scenario ever came up, to just use a bar of 2/4 to turn it around…

Ok I’m not going to waste any more time here, I’m out. I’ve tried being respectful and nice, but you just won’t get it. In your own words you describe yourself as “very much an amateur”. I’ve tried to politely make it clear that I’m not, I’m a very successful professional, who has spent a lifetime studying this stuff. I’m trying to help you grow here, but you’re totally shut off to the possibility.

You even came to me with your own stipulations and facts which you Googled to back up your point (i.e. the tempo being 80bpm in 4/4), which only proved you wrong and backed up exactly what I was saying. And you still won’t accept it. I can’t help you.
*thine presence

I’m saying it’s not any more clear the way you explain it, at least personally. The original frame of reference also was the BB landing on 2/4 (in the orig. comment) and bc everyone’s free to conceptualize it how they feel, there’s not a necessity to correct one or the other. That does make me a hypocrite, bc I thought you had it “wrong,” as to me, what you were explaing implies normal time, not half time.

I guess I just don’t see why it’s easier to feel it one way or the other, so I felt that the way it was originally said was fine and didn’t need to be changed to adhere to an “official” frame of reference, but rather whatever was already framed by the original commenter.

In conclusion, no one’s wrong as long as you’re grooving. I guess I just thought it wouldn’t help to specify one arbitrary framework over the other.

I'm not arguing about how you "should" feel it at all, if you feel it a certain way and that works, go for it, by all means. 

My point is that there's a standard way we *talk* about it, and we're being asked for advice by someone we don't know.

We all know that a shuffle is a triplet with the middle partial left out. 

So for a standard 4/4 shuffle the count is 1-a2-a3-a4-a and the snare is on 2 and 4. For a halftime the snare is on three, but the ostinato remains the same. 

I've no objection to you feeling or counting that however you want, but, this is the simplest way to communicate it, not just because of this section, but the song changes, in other parts the backbeat is on four, and it's also the standard way people do actually count it.

So it's not *quite* arbitrary. In origin maybe, but it's also the way the majority of people understand it.

The simplest thing in the world would be to count it as a 4/4 halftime shuffle with the backbeat on the 3, so you don't have to add odd measures to catch up.

The fact that you don't have to add a bar of 2/4 should prove that by itself.

You're not trying to help anyone grow here, you're trying to express your superiority, that's why you didn't actually respond to me in the first place, you just talked about me to other commenters.

My answers here actually convey the meaning and explain what's happening to people who don't understand, you're just insisting you're great. Hope that works out for you.

It just doesn’t seem like it’s clearly the simplest way to communicate it. And ya, I know you arent objecting to how I’m personally framing it, I’m saying that it’s probably more useful to explain that there are infinite ways to conceptualize it and not push toward standardizing one way or the other. Just because most people happen to follow that way doesn’t make it any less arbitrary, just that it’s a more widely accepted aribitrary system.

It's less arbitrary purely because it's conventional. If it's just me and you talking, then they're as arbitrary as each other.

But if you learned it from a book, YouTube, etc, then the way I'm talking about it is probably how they did too, so what I say matches, where while how you do it is most likely just as good, it's also more likely to result in miscommunication. 

It's also simpler because even with just this song everything fits the quarter note grid my way, but your way means some backbeats are going to land on 8th notes, because it's not all half time shuffle, some parts have backbeats on the 4 by my count, but that's the & of 2 and 4 for yours. And sometimes you'll need bars of 2/4 to fit, where that's just another bar for me.

It's not that you're wrong per se, and it's not changing the music, it's just less to think about
It’ll never sound as clean as Jeff Porcaro, that man made magic.

It’ll nev'r sound as crisp as jeff porcaro, yond sir madeth charm

***



^(I am a bot and I swapp'd some of thy words with Shakespeare words.)

Commands: `!ShakespeareInsult`, `!fordo`, `!optout`
Shakespeare-Bot, thou hast been voted most annoying bot on Reddit. I am exhorting all mods to ban thee and thy useless rhetoric so that we shall not be blotted with thy presence any longer.
Slow that shit down. You can’t play it clean at that tempo yet. Get it clean at 60 BPM and move up slowly from there.
Or play Grapevine Fires by Deathcab for Cutie without the hihat thing. Its a great mellow song for learning the half time shuffle

Get it right Slow first.
You need to get the rocking action on the hats to get the right feel
Watch Porcaro videos on YouTube

Use more of the stick and not just the tip on the hats. Don’t be afraid to use the sticks..the contact point between the drum and the stick is much more than a single point. Ghost notes and tip only and the backbeat is rim, shaft and tip on the left hand. You’re constantly adjusting the contact point to give it phrasing. The stick has 3 points of contact during a rimshot and one on a ghost..they produce very different sounds.
Seems way fast for “Rosanna”.
WHAT HAVE I DONE?
Hahahaha

I actually did a pretty thorough deep dive drum lesson into the technique for this on my YouTube channel. If you wanna check that out, it’ll help you resolve those ghost notes. https://youtu.be/MCkWvfT0rI8
I'll definitely check it out thanks lot!
So on the hi hats I should move from tip to the stick (upper half) and back to the tip kind of motion - am I making sense?
Here you go, my friend! [https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ghjyEoI-8nE](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ghjyEoI-8nE) I finally found a moment to do this for you. :)
Omg thank you so much!! This was like a hidden gem piece for me although I have to relearn my way of holding a drumstick this method got me playing much more smoother!
You're welcome! :)
Try putting the snare on 2’s and 4’s

The problem  is actually in your right hand.. when it comes time for the back beat. Your right hand should just keep on cruising forward, playing the shuffle pattern (ostensto), independent of the left hand and not behave differently during the back beat or just before..right now , your right hand is winding up , sort of 'connected' to the left hand during the back beat..this means your ostenato is not consistent rhythmically or dynamically,  which makes the ghost notes impossible to be smoith also.. you are almost there.. slow down.. way down..  play only the hands ,tryung to get the left hand to behave independently without affecting the right hands behavior...  once you can wind up the left and your right just keeps on shuffling unaffected, it will all fall into place..im short, so your independence is not quite there....
Ok.. i just realized you're a lefty... when I said right hand in my comment. I meant lead hand. So, it transpose left and right in my original comment... sorry

submission.title: I lost feelings and definitely think relationships are not my thing

comments.body: I understand. But definitely try a couple counselling or relationship coaching. Sometimes we tend to take emotion driven decisions a lot.
Well I'm 17 so...

Relax man. You're hardly old enough to understand your own feelings, let alone what you want out of a relationship, so don't be so hard on yourself.

You are still maturing emotionally and changes can be very fast and seemingly without explanation at that age, because most of it comes down to your body's chemistry. 

If you feel like you have to end it, then do it, she will be ok. Just take your lessons from every relationship and keep moving forward. Eventually things will become clearer.

For some guys this happens too if they have sex quickly with a girl and u didn’t build up to that. Or u realized she isn’t compatible with u for anything longterm after knowing her more. It’s not true about relationships not being your thing, I’m sure there’s more to it. God says in the Bible that it is not good for men to be alone. The most enduring relationships don’t last because of feelings but bc people commit themselves to a shared goal. Relationships have ups and downs and your feelings can change rapidly from time to time for your significant other but if u center your life around how God wants u to live then u will have enduring relationships bc u will understand more why this happens and can see your relationships in a more mature context. You’re still a very young man and inexperienced in life.

submission.title: Stuck and frustrated
submission.body:
I feel so frustrated when I drum now mostly cause I've been stuck at the same position for the past 2 months and I practice at least 2 hours everyday smh
My left hand's speed isn't improving and my leg speed is also not improving, in fact my knee is starting to hurt when I play quick kicks
My hand speed ain't improving either smh 
Any advise?

comments.body: Take this with as many grains of salt as you think appropriate, but there’s a lot more to drumming than speed. What are some of your favourite songs? Play along to them. Figure out the grooves and work on nailing them. Jam with people. Have fun!

Practice to a metronome. Pick a slow tempo, focus on building stamina and steady hits. Then gradually increase the tempo once you’re comfortable. Keep challenging yourself this way. Learn different rudiments and try different sticking patterns. Learn open handed techniques. 

There’s much more than speed that’s important with drumming - namely, timekeeping.

You’re developing but not in a direction that will provide any long term benefit. Your playing is like a living organism, and right now it’s growing like a weed. So taking a step back and developing things like hand positions, rudiments, counting, reading, and stick stroke development will help turn your weeds into actual functional members of your “garden”.

If something hurts that tells me there is tension. You can't build speed through tension it has to be relaxed. Additionally some people hit speed limits for certain techniques and have to use a different one. A great example is the bass pedal techniques, some people play way faster heal down and some heal up. And like someone else said speed isn't everything, I am not the fastest drummer but I get gigs because I'm more musical.

It's not the notes you play, it's the notes you don't play.

You have to work the left hand far more than the right hand right now, and plan to do that for the next couple of months. Push the muscles, but be mindful of your grip and technique. Listen to the body too: if you get pain, stop and re-evaluate your technique.

When you practice, how hard do you push the muscles? Do you work really hard on them to force them to get stronger?
When I focus on my left hand using the finger push grip I end up using my wrist and when I force myself to use the finger push grip either the stick falls or it just doesn't feel right
I think I can make a video lesson for you on grip and technique that should help you be able to get some effective muscle exercising going. It won't be today but within the next few days.

I can relate to the left hand weakness. What I did was work through joe morellos master studies. Obviously emphasizing the left hand exercise. The stone killer section is what I focused on. Also the fill-in studies section.
Thank you so much!

I made this instructional video on grip and technique yesterday for you and 9 other people. :)

[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OlaKyfKMdp4](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OlaKyfKMdp4)

I normally do a video like this for one person at a time, but I've been sitting here for 2 weeks with all these threads piling up and my life not allowing me to get around to doing the videos. So, you can consider this video to be just for you even though this one is for 9 others as well. lol

Please also watch this super short video after the main one: [https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ua4Hq0m-xBE](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ua4Hq0m-xBE) I show an exercise in this video that I forgot to show at the end of the main one.
Thanks a lot mate, I'm sorry I'm replying 2 years later. Although I did improve quite a bit from then I can still use your advice 🙌
Nah, later is always better than never! 👍I'll be here if you need any help on what I put in the video. I may not remember what I put in it, but that's ok. 😊

submission.title: ARTURIA MINILAB 3 IN ABLETON 12
submission.body:
I'm having issues with my midi or Ableton im not very sure but I'll list them out please help because I feel something changed and im not sure what. but I am STRUGGLING. I started using it again after a 2 month break.

1. When I click on program on the midi, the pads go to scenes, so it displays scene 1, scene 2. and the loop button is automatically selected, but I don't want it in the mixer view I want it in the arrangement window.

2. When I click on arpeggio, the tempo isn't in sync, it doesn't even tap tempo, and it doesn't work on pads, I have to use the keys to use arp.

3. When I click on program, I can't use the pads for beatmaking. it plays the scenes and only the recording button, play and stop button are in use.

  
I don't remember it being this annoying the last time I was using my midi with Ableton \*sob\*

comments.body: Are you hitting shift + program?

The problem of the arpeggio not syncing properly can often be linked to the MIDI settings in Ableton. Ensure that the MIDI clock settings are configured correctly. 

You may need to check whether the Minilab is set to send clock signals and that Ableton is receiving them properly. 

FYI some users have found that the arpeggio function works better when triggered from the keys rather than the pads.

submission.title: DOUBLE PEDAL HELP!!
submission.body:
My double pedal has a screw missing (I think) and for the life of me I can't figure out what screw to put in there or where to find one. And this beater keeps moving around so I can't even practice properly 😭

And the hole isn't the same size for this spare nut I got from my other pedal to fit in.

comments.body:
I never understood why people use all caps and !! in their titles..

Anyway that screw is now a spare part as long as the pedals work sufficiently.
Haha the caps are for urgency, thing is because that screw isn't there that beater keeps moving left and right. So it moves in a floppy way.

It's hard to see, but isn't that a set screw? Get out some hex keys and see if one fits in there and tighten it up. My pedal is the same. Looks like a hole but the outer edges are threaded, set screws are like low profile screws without a head.
Do you mind showing me what it looks like??

I got you man

https://imgur.com/a/74CYrKA
Ok so basically I use the Allen key to tighten it?
Yup

submission.title: Violin arpeggios
submission.body: Hi guys, does anyone have like a sheet full of arpeggios that I should learn? I'm not a beginner with the violin, I lean towards an intermediate level. But not quite there yet.

comments.body: Invest in Flesch Scales & Arpeggios.
For a cheaper, less intimidating version of scales and arpeggios that has much of what is in Flesch, you can try Barbara Barber’s Scales for Advanced Violinists.

Carl Flesch is the way to go. Get it spiral bound at a local copy shop.  
If you want to preview it, I think there are some public domain PDFs on the internet.  
If Flesch looks too difficult, there is also Hřímalý, which you can find on IMSLP. I find the organization a little overwhelming, but the scales that start on 2nd finger (mid way through the book?) are good prep for Flesch.
Oh! I will definitely get the book then! Thank you!
Flesch is the way to go! It's overwhelming on the page but it's a great resource.
Hi, I have tried finding for the books, do you have any links I can go to?

Yeah!  
Flesch: https://vmirror.imslp.org/files/imglnks/usimg/4/43/IMSLP887537-PMLP1157657-Flesch_scales_bw.pdf  
Hrimaly: https://s9.imslp.org/files/imglnks/usimg/7/78/IMSLP96393-PMLP198244-HrimalyScaleStudies.pdf
